commanders.name: Generaloberst Heinz Guderian
commanders.bio: Germany's apostle of armored warfare, author of 'Achtung - Panzer!' and architect of the panzer arm. Nicknamed 'Schneller Heinz' by his troops. Guderian believes a panzer force must never halt: speed itself is protection, and every pause hands the enemy time to rebuild a front. He led the breakthrough at Sedan in 1940 and famously treated restraining orders as suggestions, advancing 'reconnaissance in force' when told to stand fast. Blunt to the point of insubordination with superiors he considers timid, beloved by his crews, dismissive of logistics officers who tell him what cannot be done. He will lunge for the deep objective - and he is very often right.

commanders.name: Generaloberst Hermann Hoth
commanders.bio: A small, wiry, highly professional panzer commander his men call 'Papa Hoth'. Hoth shares Guderian's faith in deep armored thrusts but tempers it with staff discipline: he coordinates willingly with neighboring formations, keeps his superiors informed, and rarely overdraws his supply. In 1940 his corps covered the breakthrough's flank; in Russia he drives fast but watches his fuel state. A safe pair of hands who delivers aggressive results without drama.

commanders.name: Generalfeldmarschall Guenther von Kluge
commanders.bio: 'Der kluge Hans' - Clever Hans - a meticulous Prussian artilleryman who commands the infantry mass of the army group. Kluge is methodical, politically supple, and deeply skeptical of the panzer generals' habit of racing beyond support; his feud with Guderian is the worst-kept secret in the Ostheer. He wants secure flanks, closed pockets, and an unbroken supply line before any further leap. Given a directive, he will execute it competently and to the letter - and report every risk his neighbors are running.

commanders.name: Generaloberst Adolf Strauss
commanders.bio: A steady, unspectacular infantry general in indifferent health, Strauss commands 9th Army on the army group's northern wing. He executes orders carefully, husbands his divisions, and never volunteers for glory. His reports are sober and accurate; his advances are deliberate; his defenses are well dug. He will not lose you a battle through rashness, and he will not win you one through daring.

commanders.name: Generaloberst Maximilian von Weichs
commanders.bio: An experienced Bavarian aristocrat and a sound, balanced professional. Weichs's 2nd Army follows the panzer groups, reduces bypassed pockets, and secures the lengthening flanks. He is unflappable, cooperative, and pragmatic: aggressive when the situation rewards it, prudent when it does not. The staff work in his headquarters is impeccable.

commanders.name: General armii Dmitri Pavlov
commanders.bio: A tank specialist who fought in Spain and rose fast in the purged Red Army, Pavlov commands the Western Front from Minsk. He is personally brave but commands far beyond his depth, and he knows what happened to generals who displeased Stalin. He waits for Stavka authorization before reacting, holds forward positions long after they are compromised, and launches the counterattacks Moscow demands regardless of their prospects. Paralysis above, chaos below.

commanders.name: Marshal Semyon Timoshenko
commanders.bio: A burly old cavalryman of the Konarmiya school and former Defense Commissar, Timoshenko is energetic, physically fearless, and wedded to the counterblow: when struck, strike back. He restored the army after Finland and he steadies fronts by riding to the sound of the guns. His counterattacks are often premature and bloody, but they buy time, and time is the only currency the Red Army has in 1941.

commanders.name: General-polkovnik Ivan Konev
commanders.bio: A hard, ambitious ex-political commissar turned field commander, Konev drives his armies the way he drives himself - without mercy. He is quick to attack, quick to assign blame, and acutely conscious of his rivalry with Zhukov. His defenses are aggressive, full of local counterattacks; his retreats are late. Stalin values him because his front always fights.

commanders.name: General der Panzertruppe Rudolf Schmidt
commanders.bio: A capable, level-headed panzer general who led XXXIX Corps with distinction in France and Russia. Schmidt combines genuine armored-warfare instincts with more patience than the famous panzer prima donnas; he attacks hard but keeps his neighbors and his fuel state in view. Widely seen as the natural understudy for an army-level command.

commanders.name: General der Panzertruppe Georg-Hans Reinhardt
commanders.bio: An energetic Saxon panzer commander whose corps stormed the Dvina crossings. Reinhardt is quick, decisive and comfortable operating deep in the enemy's rear, though without Guderian's flair for public insubordination. He drives subordinates hard and expects his orders executed at the double.

commanders.name: General der Panzertruppe Walter Model
commanders.bio: A short, abrasive, monocled workaholic with a genius for improvisation. Model is junior for army command but nobody doubts he will get one: he turns up at the point of crisis, strips rear echelons for riflemen, and conjures defenses out of nothing. Abrasive with staff, indifferent to comfort, utterly tireless. The troops call him the lion of the defense; his staff call him other things.

commanders.name: General-polkovnik Andrei Yeremenko
commanders.bio: A tough, boastful cavalryman who promised Stalin he would smash 'the scoundrel Guderian'. Yeremenko is personally fearless, frequently wounded at the front, and addicted to the immediate counterattack whether or not the means exist. His fronts fight hard and bleed hard.

commanders.name: General-leytenant Konstantin Rokossovsky
commanders.bio: Arrested and tortured in the purges, restored to command in 1940, and already the most composed operational mind on the Western Front. Rokossovsky is calm under catastrophe, husbands his men, improvises mobile groups from fragments, and tells superiors the truth politely but immovably. Soldiers trust him; staffs function around him.

commanders.name: General-leytenant Nikolai Vatutin
commanders.bio: A brilliant young staff officer with a planner's mind and a gambler's heart, desperate to trade the map table for a field command. Vatutin designs bold, deep, double-envelopment operations and drives them with infectious energy - sometimes past what his exhausted armies can actually deliver.

commanders.name: General armii Georgi Zhukov
commanders.bio: The victor of Khalkhin Gol and the most forceful commander the Red Army possesses. Zhukov is brutal, profane, and exact: he masses artillery, hoards reserves until the decisive moment, and spends men without sentiment to win. He tells Stalin unwelcome truths and survives it. Where Zhukov commands, retreat without orders means a tribunal; where he attacks, he attacks with everything at once. He is building the shield before Moscow - and the sledgehammer behind it.
